title: Embryo at week 5: neural crest migration
vignette: A 24-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a missed menstrual period. She has had regular menstrual cycles every 28 days, and her last menstrual period began 7 weeks ago. Since then, she had sexual intercourse once 5 weeks ago. The patient has no chronic medical conditions. Urine pregnancy test is positive. Which of the following embryologic events is most likely occurring in the embryo at this time?

clues.text: her last menstrual period began 7 weeks ago
clues.note: Gestational age is dated from the last menstrual period (LMP = 7 weeks). But fertilization/conception occurs about 2 weeks AFTER the LMP, so embryonic (post-conception) age is younger than the gestational age.
Conception happened ~5 weeks ago, so the embryo is about 5 weeks post-fertilization. At ~week 5 post-conception, the embryo is in organogenesis with active neural crest cell migration.

whatMatters: Date by CONCEPTION, not LMP: intercourse 5 weeks ago means the embryo is ~5 weeks post-fertilization. At that stage (organogenesis, weeks 3-8), neural crest cells are migrating to form their many derivatives. Implantation (week 1) and gastrulation (week 3) are already past; alveoli form much later.
strategy: For 'what's happening in the embryo now,' convert to POST-CONCEPTION age (subtract ~2 weeks from LMP, or use the intercourse date). Then map to the embryology timeline: implantation wk 1, gastrulation wk 3, neural crest migration/organogenesis wks 4-8, fetal growth later.

eliminations.label: implantation — week 1, already past
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the blastocyst implants into the endometrium about 6-7 days after fertilization (week 1). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: at ~5 weeks post-conception this happened over a month ago; it's far too early an event. ✅ Correct if: the embryo were within the first week post-fertilization.

eliminations.label: gastrulation — week 3, already past
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: gastrulation (formation of the 3 germ layers via the primitive streak) occurs in week 3 post-conception. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it precedes neural crest migration and is finished by ~week 5; not the current event. ✅ Correct if: the embryo were ~3 weeks post-fertilization.

eliminations.label: intestinal fixation — much later (weeks 10-11+)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: midgut rotation and fixation to the posterior abdominal wall occur after physiologic herniation, around weeks 10-12. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that's well beyond a 5-week embryo; too late for this stage. ✅ Correct if: the question concerned late first-trimester midgut rotation/fixation.

eliminations.label: primitive alveoli — late fetal (weeks 24+)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: alveolar/saccular lung development occurs in the late second to third trimester (alveoli mature even into infancy). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's a fetal-period event, nowhere near a 5-week embryo. ✅ Correct if: the fetus were in the late second/third trimester (saccular-alveolar stage).

reasoning: LMP 7 weeks ago, but conception was ~5 weeks ago (intercourse date)
At this stage, neural crest cells are actively migrating
mechanism: Pregnancy dating uses gestational age (from LMP), which is ~2 weeks more than the post-conception (embryonic) age. Intercourse 5 weeks ago places the embryo at ~5 weeks post-fertilization, within organogenesis (weeks 3-8) — the period of greatest teratogenic susceptibility. During this window neural crest cells delaminate from the dorsal neural tube and migrate to form PNS neurons/Schwann cells, melanocytes, adrenal medulla, craniofacial bones, and more. Implantation and gastrulation are earlier; intestinal fixation and alveoli are later.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Embryology > timeline: week 1 implantation, week 2 bilaminar, week 3 gastrulation/neurulation begins, weeks 3-8 organogenesis (neural crest migration), later fetal events. Memorize the early-week milestones.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Translate dates: use the conception date (intercourse) for embryonic age, not LMP. Then place ~5 weeks on the timeline (organogenesis → neural crest migration).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on gestational vs post-conception age, gastrulation, neural crest, organogenesis, implantation? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: LMP '7 weeks' baits you to overshoot; implantation/gastrulation are the 'too early' traps and alveoli/intestinal fixation the 'too late' traps. Anchor on conception = 5 weeks → neural crest migration.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference is the fetal-development/teratogenesis timeline figure. Use it to locate week-5 events and the highly teratogen-sensitive organogenesis window.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.back: PNS/Schwann, melanocytes, adrenal medulla, craniofacial bones (MOTEL PASS)

firstAid.detail: Embryology > weeks of development: implantation, gastrulation, neurulation/neural crest migration, organogenesis window; gestational vs embryonic age.
firstAid.location: Embryology > weeks of development: implantation, gastrulation, neurulation/neural crest migration, organogenesis window; gestational vs embryonic age.

firstAid.detail: Embryology > neural crest table (MOTEL PASS) — what neural crest migration ultimately forms.
firstAid.location: Embryology > neural crest table (MOTEL PASS) — what neural crest migration ultimately forms.

firstAid.detail: Embryology > teratogen sensitivity: organogenesis (weeks 3-8) = structural-anomaly window; later = functional defects.
firstAid.location: Embryology > teratogen sensitivity: organogenesis (weeks 3-8) = structural-anomaly window; later = functional defects.

memoryHook: Date by the bedroom, not the calendar: conception 5 weeks ago → organogenesis → neural crest cells on the move.
